column1: Oberlappen: Yeah I think my judgement should be better, as one of my biggest problems.
Ten: How well that influence converts to territory decides the result of the game, but we know that influence is best used for attacking, not for forming territory
Ten: I've also noticed in your NGD games that you like to play unconventional moves, I think that's in general a very good thing :)
Ten: It both keeps the game more interesting, and also actually can be very efficient, since then your opponents cannot rely on memorised patterns
Oberlappen: To be honest, it´s about finding new ways.
Ten: But in exchange, you need to be able to generate 'efficient unconventional moves', and for that you need to have good judgement skills

Ten: Anyway it becomes a bit like you're just copying something from a book to the go board
Ten: I didn't used to be satisfied with that either
Ten: My own way to finally go about it is that, in the end, I play fairly common moves, but I combine them into uncommon strategies
Ten: A bit like, if you compare 'shapes' on the go board to words, then 'strategies' are like sentences
Oberlappen: Thats logical, have benefits from both ways.
Ten: So I don't find it necessary to come up with my own words all the time, but the way I arrange them into sentences makes a big impact
Ten: But, well, it's not like that's the only way to think about it :)
Oberlappen: Yeah the order is so important in Go.

C[SekiThisIs: Is this really a good choice of joseki for white, given the black stone at R11 and all?
C[Ten: My thinking was actually 'this is a good choice of joseki for white BECAUSE black has a stone at r11'
Ten: In the end, go is a game of surrounding territory, but for the most part of a game it is actually more important to concern about the status of your groups rather than surrounding territory
Ten: White's argument with this joseki choice is that, because black ends up with a strong shape (the ponnuki), the r11 stone becomes a bit superfluous because it close to the strong r6 group

column1: Ten: Instead of n12, it's much easier and safer to play m16, linking the black stones up with good shape

column1: Ten: Instead of k15, probably drawing the f14 stone out with g14 would work, but black doesn't really need to do that anyway to secure his win
Ten: For example instead of k18, if you noticed white has a living sequence in the upper-left corner and just killed it with f18, then black would win by a wide margin
Ten: finally when white got to live in the corner and black didn't have a big move to play in response, the score got a lot closer, but even then black has way enough
column2: so most likely a four-stone game would have been more appropriate
